Idiomatic Expressions

  • 家貧 - Indicates the narrator's humble background, common in classical Chinese literature.
  • 游学 - Refers to the practice of traveling to study, highly valued in ancient China.
  • 温习《仪礼注疏》 - Indicates the narrator's scholarly activities, specifically reviewing the commentary and annotations on *Yili* (Rites of Zhou).
  • 有白额虎衔之而去 - A vivid description of a white-furred tiger carrying away the narrator, likely a metaphorical or symbolic event.
  • 乃掷于深谷中 - Means 'then threw it into a deep valley,' where 'it' likely refers to the narrator.
  • 虎竟去 - Means 'the tiger finally left,' indicating the end of the encounter.
  • 心悔 - Means 'felt regret,' expressing the narrator's emotional state.
  • 当是背宋儒之报也 - Means 'perhaps this is the retribution for rejecting the teachings of the Song Confucians,' suggesting a moral or spiritual consequence.
  • 方懊恼间 - Means 'just as he was feeling懊恼,' where '懊恼' means 'disappointed' or 'frustrated.'
  • 见谷旁有石大门开 - Means 'saw a stone gate open on the side of the valley,' indicating a sudden and unexpected event.
  • 邱走入 - Means 'Qiu entered,' where '邱' is the narrator's name.
  • 殿宇巍峨 - Means 'grand and magnificent halls,' describing the impressive architecture.
  • 署曰“文明殿” - Means 'the sign read 'Civilization Hall',' indicating the name of the place.
  • 两旁罗列书籍百万 - Means 'millions of books were lined up on both sides,' emphasizing the vast number of books.
  • 莫知其数 - Means 'no one knows the number,' reinforcing the vastness of the collection.
  • 掀翻书目 - Means 'flipped through the book catalog,' indicating the narrator's attempt to find certain books.
  • 谓必以六经冠首 - Means 'thought that the *Six Classics* would be at the beginning,' expecting the most important texts to be listed first.
  • 不意翻毕，竟无有也 - Means 'unexpectantly, after flipping through, there were none,' indicating disappointment.
  • 心疑之 - Means 'felt doubtful,' expressing the narrator's confusion.
  • 旁有古衣冠者倚门而立 - Means 'there was an anciently dressed person leaning against the door,' describing a mysterious figure.
  • 揖而问曰 - Means 'bowed and asked,' indicating a formal and respectful greeting.
  • 此处何神所居 - Means 'who is the god who resides here,' asking about the identity of the deity.
  • 苍圣 - Means 'the ancient sage,' referring to a revered figure.
  • 始制文字 - Means 'originally created writing,' indicating the creation of writing systems.
  • 自该万卷横陈 - Means 'should have countless volumes laid out,' suggesting the expected abundance of books.
  • 独无古《六经》何耶 - Means 'why are there no ancient *Six Classics*,' expressing surprise and questioning.
  • 向来原有此书 - Means 'there used to be these books,' indicating a historical presence.
  • 但名《诗》《书》《周易》，不名经也 - Means 'but they were called *Shi* (Poetry), *Shu* (Documents), *Zhouyi* (Changes), not *Jing* (Classics),' explaining the naming convention.
  • 自汉人多事 - Means 'since the Han people did many things,' indicating a historical context.
  • 名曰《六经》 - Means 'named them *Six Classics*,' referring to the renaming of the texts.
  • 造作注疏 - Means 'created annotations and commentaries,' indicating the addition of scholarly work.
  • 穿凿附会 - Means 'forced connections and interpretations,' criticizing the scholarly practices.
  • 致动上帝之怒 - Means 'caused the anger of the Supreme Deity,' suggesting divine retribution.
  • 责苍圣造字生此厉阶 - Means 'blamed the ancient sage for creating writing, which led to this source of trouble,' indicating the cause of divine displeasure.
  • 从此，文明殿中撤去注疏 - Means 'from then on, the annotations and commentaries were removed from the Civilization Hall,' indicating a change in the library's contents.
  • 致汝掀翻不得 - Means 'causing you to be unable to flip through them,' explaining the narrator's frustration.
  • 注疏何以上干天怒 - Means 'why did the annotations and commentaries cause the anger of the Supreme Deity,' asking for an explanation.
  • 此事原委甚长 - Means 'the details of this matter are very long,' indicating the complexity of the story.
  • 汝且静听我言 - Means 'you should listen quietly to my words,' advising the narrator to pay attention.
  • 汝可知万国九州，只有一天乎 - Means 'do you know that there is only one sky over all the nations and regions,' asking a fundamental question.
  • 自盘古开辟以来 - Means 'since Pangu created the world,' referring to the mythological beginning of the world.
  • 三皇五帝 - Refers to the legendary rulers of ancient China.
  • 钦若昊天 - Means 'revered the Supreme Deity,' indicating respect and worship.
  • 天亦安享郊牛 - Means 'the sky also enjoyed the sacrifices of oxen,' indicating the reciprocal relationship between humans and deities.
  • 数千年矣 - Means 'for thousands of years,' indicating a long period of time.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 家貧 - Indicates the narrator's humble background, which is common in classical Chinese literature.
  • 游学 - Reflects the traditional practice of traveling to study, which was highly valued in ancient China.
  • 温习《仪礼注疏》 - Refers to the *Yili* (Rites of Zhou) and its commentary, which were important texts in Confucian education.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 凡郑所奏，帝亦颁行世间 - Formal statement indicating that whatever Zheng proposed, the emperor also issued to the world, reflecting the hierarchical relationship between the emperor and his officials.
  • 久之，其教有必不能行者 - After a long time, it became evident that some of the teachings could not be implemented, highlighting the passage of time and the practical limitations of certain doctrines.
  • 天子冕旒用玉二百八十八片 - The emperor’s crown uses 288 pieces of jade, emphasizing the grandeur and complexity of imperial regalia.
  • 天子之头几乎压死 - The emperor’s head is almost crushed, a hyperbolic statement underscoring the weight and symbolism of the crown.
  • 夏祭地示必服大裘 - In the summer, when sacrificing to the earth, the emperor must wear a heavy fur coat, highlighting the ritualistic nature of the emperor’s duties.
  • 天子之身几乎死 - The emperor’s body is almost suffocated, a hyperbole emphasizing the discomfort and impracticality of the ritual.
  • 只许每日一食，须劝再食 - The emperor is only allowed one meal a day, but he must be persuaded to eat a second time, illustrating the strict dietary regulations and the pressure on the emperor to adhere to them.
  • 天子之腹几乎饿死 - The emperor’s stomach is almost starved, a hyperbole emphasizing the severity of the dietary restrictions.
  • 丧礼，含殓用米二升四合 - In funeral rites, two sheng and four he of rice are used for the mouth and body, highlighting the meticulous nature of funeral rituals.
  • 君大夫口含梁稷四升 - The lord and high officials put four sheng of millet and wheat into their mouths, emphasizing the hierarchical differences in funeral rites.
  • 如角四不能启其齿，则凿尸颊一小穴而纳之 - If the jaw cannot be opened, a small hole is drilled in the cheek to insert the food, a practice both gruesome and ritualistic, reflecting the extreme measures taken to ensure proper funeral rites.
  • 凡为子孙者，心俱不忍 - All descendants feel heartache, expressing the emotional toll of these rituals on the living.
  • 以讹传讹，习而不察，将及千年 - Misunderstandings spread, and over time, they become accepted, lasting nearly a thousand years, highlighting the persistence of errors and the difficulty of correcting them.
  • 一日，天帝坐紫薇宫，见云中飞下一兽来 - One day, the Heavenly Emperor sat in the Purple Palace and saw a creature fly down from the clouds, introducing a mythological element, setting the stage for the supernatural encounter.
  • 龙鳞马鬣，喊冤奏曰 - The creature has dragon scales and a horse mane, and it cries out in complaint, emphasizing the creature’s hybrid nature and its emotional state.
  • 臣麒麟也，不食生虫，不践恶草 - I am a qilin, and I do not eat living insects or tread on evil grass, self-identifying and describing the qilin’s characteristics, highlighting its noble and virtuous nature.
  • 人人称为仁兽，必待圣人出，臣才下世 - Everyone calls me a benevolent beast, and I wait for a sage to appear before descending to the world, emphasizing the qilin’s role as a harbinger of a sage and its connection to moral and spiritual purity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 郑 - Refers to Zheng, a historical figure or official who proposed certain teachings or rituals.
  • 帝 - Refers to the emperor, the supreme ruler of the state.
  • 冕旒 - The imperial crown, a symbol of the emperor’s authority and divine right to rule.
  • 夏祭地示 - The summer sacrifice to the earth, a ritual performed by the emperor to honor the earth deity.
  • 大裘 - A heavy fur coat, typically worn by the emperor during winter sacrifices.
  • 丧礼 - Funeral rites, which were highly ritualized and symbolic in ancient China.
  • 含殓 - The practice of placing items in the mouth and body of the deceased during the funeral.
  • 君大夫 - High-ranking officials or nobles.
  • 紫薇宫 - The Purple Palace, a mythical or symbolic place often associated with the emperor or the heavens.
  • 麒麟 - A mythical creature often associated with sages and moral virtue, believed to appear only in times of peace and prosperity.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence introduces the context of Zheng’s proposals and the emperor’s actions, setting the stage for the subsequent discussion.
  • This clause indicates a shift in time and the realization that some teachings are impractical.
  • This specific detail about the imperial crown is followed by a hyperbolic statement about its weight.
  • This ritual is described in a way that seems counterintuitive, highlighting the ritualistic nature of the practice.
  • This dietary regulation is followed by a hyperbolic statement about the emperor’s hunger, emphasizing the strictness of the rules.
  • This specific detail about the funeral rites is followed by a description of the high officials’ practices, emphasizing the hierarchical differences.
  • This gruesome detail is followed by a statement about the emotional impact on descendants, highlighting the ritual’s intensity.
  • This phrase describes the persistence of misunderstandings and the difficulty of correcting them.
  • This sentence introduces a mythological element, setting the stage for the supernatural encounter.
  • This description of the creature is followed by its self-identification and explanation of its characteristics, emphasizing its noble nature.

  • 紫薇宫 - The Purple Palace.
  • 麒麟 - The qilin, a mythical creature.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 不料有妄人郑某、孔某者 - Phrase meaning 'unfortunately, there are foolish people named Zheng and Kong who,' indicating the introduction of unreliable individuals.
  • 生造注疏 - Means 'fabricated annotations,' suggesting that Zheng and Kong created false interpretations.
  • 剥麒麟之皮蒙鼓 - Literal expression meaning 'peel the skin of a qilin and cover it on a drum,' used metaphorically to describe an absurd ritual.
  • 信如所言 - Means 'if what they say is true,' introducing a hypothetical scenario.
  • 遭此屠毒 - Means 'suffer such slaughter,' expressing the cruelty of the situation.
  • 只好吓骗黄巾贼 - Means 'only suitable for scaring and deceiving the Yellow Turban rebels,' referring to a group of rebels during the late Eastern Han period.
  • 见老郑便一齐下拜 - Means 'seeing Old Zheng, they all prostrate themselves,' indicating the absurdity of the situation.
  • 使麒麟见之，必唾其面 - Means 'if a qilin saw them, it would spit on their faces,' further emphasizing the absurdity.
  • 言未毕 - Means 'before the words were finished,' indicating the continuation of the narrative.
  • 空中云鬟霞佩 - Describes a woman with hair like clouds and a sash like a rainbow, a poetic way to describe a celestial or divine figure.
  • 率领数妇人姗姗来者 - Means 'leading several women who came slowly,' describing the arrival of these women.
  • 跪奏曰 - Means 'prostrate themselves and report,' indicating a formal and respectful manner of speaking.
  • 妾姜氏，周王妃也 - Means 'I am Lady Jiang, the wife of the Zhou king,' identifying the speaker.
  • 当时周王劝农 - Means 'when the Zhou king encouraged farming,' referring to a historical event.
  • 妾并不随行 - Means 'I did not accompany him,' indicating the speaker's absence.
  • 今有妄人郑某 - Means 'now there are foolish people named Zheng,' continuing the criticism.
  • 说天子劝农，必与王后同行 - Means 'who say that the emperor encouraging farming must go with the empress,' describing a false belief.
  • 妾想妇人幽闺弱质 - Means 'I think women are delicate and confined to the inner chambers,' expressing the speaker's view on women's roles.
  • 行不逾阈 - Means 'do not step beyond the threshold,' indicating the limited mobility of women.
  • 披霜冒雪出来劝农之理 - Means 'the reason for going out in the cold and snow to encourage farming,' questioning the practicality.
  • 北魏王肃曾言其非 - Means 'the Northern Wei Wang Su once said it was wrong,' citing a historical figure.
  • 唐人孔颖达将王大加呵斥 - Means 'the Tang scholar Kong Yingda harshly criticized Wang,' indicating a historical debate.
  • 党同诬妄 - Means 'favoring the same side and spreading false accusations,' describing the behavior of those involved.
  • 一至于此 - Means 'to such an extent,' emphasizing the severity of the situation.
  • 诸妇人齐奏曰 - Means 'the other women also reported,' indicating a collective response.
  • 妾南国诸侯大夫王妻也 - Means 'I am the wife of a nobleman from the southern states,' identifying the speaker.
  • 夫君外出，妾等心忧 - Means 'my husband is away, and we are worried,' expressing concern.
  • 亦既觏止，我心则降 - Means 'seeing him, my heart is at ease,' indicating relief upon seeing the husband.
  • 此人情也 - Means 'this is human nature,' concluding the sentiment.

  • 不料后来俗儒流弊，一至于斯 - Means 'unfortunately, later vulgar scholars have led to such a state of affairs.'

  • 臣所造之字都是下行 - Means 'the characters I created all go downward.'
  • 臣弟沮诵、亻去卢所造之字或右行、或左行 - Means 'my younger brothers, Ju Song and Qu Lu, created characters that go either to the right or to the left.'
  • 左右行者，行于东西二方 - Means 'characters that go to the right or left are used in the eastern and western regions.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 文王 - King Wen of Zhou, a revered ruler in Chinese history.
  • 周公 - Duke of Zhou, a key figure in the early Zhou dynasty.
  • 孔子 - Confucius, the founder of Confucianism.
  • 隋宫 - Refers to the palace during the Sui dynasty.
  • 开元宫女六万余人 - Refers to the large number of palace women during the Tang dynasty's Kaiyuan era.
  • 《诗经》 - The *Book of Songs*, one of the Five Classics.
  • 景十三王传 - Refers to the biography of the 13th king of the Jing dynasty.
  • 三代 - Refers to the Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties.
  • 天帝 - The Heavenly Emperor, a deity in Chinese mythology.
  • 苍圣 - A divine sage, possibly referring to a mythological figure.
  • 沮诵、亻去卢 - Mythological figures who are said to have helped create characters.
  • 明心见性 - A Buddhist term meaning 'to realize one's true nature.'
  • 学佛未成者 - Refers to those who have not yet completed their Buddhist studies.
  • 大显神通 - Means 'to show great supernatural powers.'
  • 一扫而空之 - Means 'to eliminate completely.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 召佛是矣 - Summoning Buddha is correct; may be a metaphor or specific context.
  • 何以要召学佛未成者 - Why must one summon those who have not yet completed their study of Buddhism; idiomatic and requires careful interpretation.
  • 佛无夫妻父子 - Literal translation of 'Buddha has no husband, wife, or father'; may carry deeper philosophical or cultural meaning.
  • 故名异端 - Therefore, it is called heretical; negative connotation.
  • 恐来中国，人多不服 - Fearing that if it comes to China, many people will not accept it; concern about cultural acceptance.
  • 惟有少时借佛书参究一番 - Only by briefly borrowing Buddhist texts to study them; suggests a limited engagement with Buddhist texts.
  • 中年同归周、孔者 - Those who, in middle age, return to Confucius and Zhou; indicates a return to traditional Chinese values.
  • 墨行儒名 - Acting like a Mohist but named as a Confucian; suggests a blend of different philosophical schools.
  • 人才肯服 - People will then be willing to accept it; indicates a shift in attitude.
  • 宋朝某某最佳 - The best ones from the Song dynasty; '某某' is a placeholder for specific names or individuals.
  • 麒麟在旁争之 - The Qilin (a mythical creature) argued against it; indicates a metaphorical or allegorical figure.
  • 楚固失矣，而齐亦未为得也 - Chu certainly lost, but Qi was not right either; historical reference to the states of Chu and Qi.
  • 不过麒麟晦气，而天帝尚得一顿饱餐 - It's just that the Qilin brings bad luck, but the Heavenly Emperor still gets a good meal; metaphorical statement.
  • 恐尖嘴雷神还要来闹 - Fearing that the sharp-nosed thunder god will still cause trouble; metaphorical reference.
  • 敬主人之礼，非以其馔也 - Respecting the host's courtesy, not because of the food; philosophical statement.
  • 迅雷必变 - When there is a sudden thunder, one must change; phrase from the *I Ching* (Book of Changes).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 佛 - Refers to Buddhism, a major religion and philosophy in China.
  • 周、孔 - Refers to Confucius and the Zhou dynasty, representing traditional Chinese values.
  • 墨行儒名 - Refers to the Mohist school of thought, which is often contrasted with Confucianism.
  • 宋朝 - Refers to the Song dynasty, a period of significant cultural and intellectual development in China.
  • 麒麟 - A mythical creature in Chinese culture, often symbolizing good fortune and virtue.
  • 楚 - Refers to the state of Chu during the Warring States period.
  • 齐 - Refers to the state of Qi during the same period.
  • 汉儒 - Refers to scholars of the Han dynasty, known for their interpretations of Confucian classics.
  • 朱注 - Refers to Zhu Xi's commentaries on the *Four Books* and *Five Classics*.
  • 迅雷必变 - A phrase from the *I Ching*, emphasizing the need for change in response to sudden events.

  • 麒麟 - A mythical creature in Chinese culture.
  • 楚 - The state of Chu during the Warring States period.
  • 齐 - The state of Qi during the same period.
  • 汉儒 - Scholars of the Han dynasty.
  • 朱注 - Zhu Xi's commentaries on the *Four Books* and *Five Classics*.
  • 迅雷必变 - A phrase from the *I Ching*.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 岂非下文暗藏不以其雷耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Is it not that the following text secretly implies something different about the thunder?'
  • 雷公没人怕了 - Colloquial expression meaning 'No one fears the Thunder God anymore', indicating a loss of respect or fear for the Thunder God.
  • 天帝笑曰 - Formal expression meaning 'The Heavenly Emperor smiled and said', introducing a divine or authoritative voice.
  • 汝言亦是 - Polite response meaning 'What you say is true', acknowledging the validity of someone's statement.
  • 气运各有盛衰 - Philosophical expression meaning 'Fates and fortunes have their ups and downs', suggesting the cyclical nature of fate and fortune.
  • 朕亦不能作主 - Formal statement meaning 'I cannot control it either', indicating a sense of helplessness or inability to influence the situation.
  • 姑且召明心见性之人 - Formal expression meaning 'Let us summon those who are enlightened and see their true nature', referring to wise and virtuous individuals.
  • 试其伎俩何如 - Formal expression meaning 'Let us test their skills and see how they fare', indicating a desire to evaluate someone's abilities.
  • 俄见苍圣带领宋儒上殿 - Formal expression meaning 'Soon, the sage led the Song scholars onto the hall', describing a scene where wise figures appear.
  • 褒衣博冠手执太极圈者 - Formal description meaning 'Those with wide sleeves and broad hats holding the Tai Chi circle', referring to scholars or sages.
  • 闭目指心自称常惺惺者 - Formal description meaning 'Those who close their eyes, point to their hearts, and claim to be always alert', indicating a state of constant awareness or mindfulness.
  • 拈花弄月自号活泼泼地者 - Formal description meaning 'Those who pick flowers, play with the moon, and call themselves lively and vibrant', describing scholars who are carefree and joyful.
  • 最后四人扛一大桶 - Formal description meaning 'Finally, four people carried a large bucket', describing a specific scene.
  • 此稻桶也 - Formal statement meaning 'This is a rice bucket', identifying the object being carried.
  • 自孔孟亡后，无人能扛此桶 - Formal statement meaning 'Since the deaths of Confucius and Mencius, no one can carry this bucket', indicating a loss of wisdom or virtue.
  • 唐人韩愈妄想扛桶 - Formal statement meaning 'The Tang scholar Han Yu foolishly tried to carry the bucket', criticizing someone for overestimating their abilities.
  • 被我取他与大颠和尚书札，搜出真赃 - Formal statement meaning 'I took his letters with the Great Venerable Monk and found the real evidence', indicating a successful investigation.
  • 把他所扛之桶多掀翻了 - Formal statement meaning 'I overturned many of the buckets he carried', indicating a punishment or reprimand.
  • 何况郑、孔，敢与我四人为难乎 - Formal statement meaning 'How much more so would Zheng and Kong dare to cause trouble for us?', indicating a challenge to others.
  • 赤怒、白招拒五妖神爬墙穴洞，偃旗息鼓而逃 - Formal description meaning 'The five evil spirits, Red Furious and White Resisting, climbed walls and dug holes, and fled with lowered flags and silenced drums', describing a scene of retreat.
  • 天帝大喜，即命此四人权摄文明殿功曹 - Formal statement meaning 'The Heavenly Emperor was greatly pleased and immediately appointed these four as temporary officials of the Hall of Civilization', indicating a reward.
  • 此汉学所以不昌，而文明殿之所以无注务也 - Formal statement meaning 'This is why Han learning has not flourished, and why the Hall of Civilization has no duties', indicating a cause-and-effect relationship.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宋儒 - Scholars of the Song Dynasty, known for their contributions to Confucianism and philosophy.

  • 赤怒、白招拒 - Names of five evil spirits, likely fictional or mythological figures.
  • 文明殿 - The Hall of Civilization, a symbolic place in Chinese mythology or literature, often associated with learning and culture.
  • 汉学 - Han learning, referring to the study of Confucianism and other classical Chinese texts.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 既如此 - Transitional phrase meaning 'since it is so' or 'given that.'

  • 一误岂容再误 - Phrase meaning 'one mistake cannot be allowed to be repeated,' emphasizing the gravity of errors.
  • 终不能久 - Phrase meaning 'cannot last long,' suggesting impermanence.
  • 与为难 - Phrase meaning 'to challenge' or 'to make difficult,' indicating opposition.
  • 忽闻钟彭声 - Phrase meaning 'suddenly hearing the sound of bells and drums,' often used to describe a sudden interruption or call to attention.
  • 苍圣传旨 - Phrase meaning 'the ancient sage conveys an imperial decree,' indicating a divine or authoritative command.
  • 白虎驮邱生来 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'the white tiger carries Qiu to come,' suggesting a supernatural or divine intervention.
  • 自矜汉学 - Phrase meaning 'to boast about Han studies,' indicating pride in one's knowledge.
  • 凌蔑百家 - Phrase meaning 'to look down on all schools of thought,' indicating arrogance and disrespect.
  • 挟天子以令诸侯 - Phrase meaning 'to use the emperor to command the feudal lords,' indicating the misuse of power.
  • 投畀豺虎 - Phrase from the Book of Songs, meaning 'to cast into the jaws of tigers,' indicating severe punishment or abandonment.
  • 可赐山中云雾茶一杯 - Phrase meaning 'can be given a cup of misty tea from the mountains,' suggesting a reward or favor.
  • 领其出山 - Phrase meaning 'to lead him out of the mountains,' indicating release or liberation.
  • 俾述所闻 - Phrase meaning 'to make him recount what he has heard,' indicating a request for a report.
  • 可以晓世 - Phrase meaning 'to enlighten the world,' indicating the purpose of the report.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 然则 - Conjunction meaning 'then' or 'thus,' used to introduce a conclusion or a new topic.
  • 好時文八股 - Refers to liking the style of eight-legged essays, a type of essay popular during the Ming and Qing dynasties.
  • 古衣冠者 - Refers to an ancient person dressed in traditional attire, often used metaphorically to represent a wise or knowledgeable figure.
  • 大笑曰 - Phrase indicating a loud laugh followed by a statement.
  • 上帝非秀才 - Statement that God is not a scholar, implying that God does not need the conventional scholarly pursuits.
  • 時文 - Refers to the style of eight-legged essays.
  • 科甲中 - Refers to those who passed the imperial examinations.
  • 豪杰 - Refers to heroes or outstanding individuals.
  • 功令所束 - Refers to being bound by the rules and regulations of the imperial examinations.
  • 吕蒙拔于盗贼 - Refers to Lu Meng, a general who rose from a life of crime to become a hero.
  • 郭子仪起于缧绁 - Refers to Guo Ziyi, a general who was once imprisoned but later became a hero.
  • 钓之可得，射之可得，网之亦可得 - Metaphorical expression indicating that heroes can be discovered through various means.
  • 大者蛟鳌，小者鲂鲤 - Refers to large and small fish, used metaphorically to represent different types of people.
  • 水所生 - Refers to people being born from the same source, implying that everyone has the potential to become a hero.
  • 不因钓射网罟而有异焉 - Indicates that the method of discovery does not change the inherent qualities of the person.
  • 历代以经学取为名臣者，若而人 - Refers to those who became famous officials through the study of Confucian classics.
  • 以诗赋策论取为名臣者，若而人 - Refers to those who became famous officials through poetry, essays, and policy discussions.
  • 以时文取为名臣者，若而人 - Refers to those who became famous officials through the study of eight-legged essays.
  • 豪杰之士，岂为功令所束而遂淹没哉 - Rhetorical question indicating that heroes are not bound by the rules and regulations of the imperial examinations.
  • 上帝何好 - Question asking what God likes.
  • 好诗文 - Refers to liking poetry and literature.
  • 汝试想上帝白玉楼成 - Rhetorical question asking the reader to imagine God's palace being completed.
  • 召老成人马季常、井大春作记 - Refers to summoning experienced scholars like Ma Jichang and Jing Dachun to write a record.
  • 召一少年挑达之李长吉 - Refers to summoning the young and lively Li He.
  • 海上仙龛，芙蓉城主 - Refers to the abode of immortals and the ruler of the city of lotus flowers.
  • 召周、程、张、朱聚徒讲学者居之 - Refers to summoning scholars like Zhou Dunyi, Cheng Hao, Zhang Zai, and Zhu Xi to teach and live there.

  • 豪纵不羁之石曼卿 - Refers to Shi Manqing, a poet known for his unrestrained and free-spirited nature.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 恍然大悟 - Common idiom meaning 'suddenly enlightened' or 'suddenly realized.'
  • 再拜 - Formal act of bowing twice, expressing deep respect or gratitude.
  • 如神人所言 - Literally 'as the divine one says,' used to introduce a statement attributed to a higher power or authority.
  • 首鼠两端 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to waver between two opinions' or 'to be indecisive.'
  • 胸中无物 - Literally 'nothing in one's heart,' meaning 'to lack substance' or 'to be empty-headed.'
  • 附物而后有所表彰 - Literally 'to attach oneself to something in order to have something to praise,' meaning 'to rely on external support to gain recognition.'
  • 天性中本无所有 - Literally 'nothing inherent in one's nature,' meaning 'one's natural disposition is lacking.'
  • 康成之注释 - Refers to the annotations of Kong Yingda, a renowned scholar of the Han dynasty.
  • 溲溲 - An archaic term, possibly referring to a process of winnowing or sifting grain.
  • 舂之播之 - Literally 'to pound and scatter,' referring to the process of grinding grain.
  • 使趋于凿 - Literally 'to make it tend toward the凿,' meaning 'to refine it into凿.'
  • 粟一石为粝 - Literally 'one dan of millet becomes coarse,' referring to the first stage of grain processing.
  • 舂一斗为稗 - Literally 'one dou of millet becomes稗,' referring to the second stage of grain processing.
  • 又去八升为凿 - Literally 'remove eight sheng to become凿,' referring to the third stage of grain processing.
  • 又去九升为侍御 - Literally 'remove nine sheng to become侍御,' referring to the final stage of grain processing.
  • 侍御者，王所食也 - Literally '侍御 is what the king eats,' meaning '侍御 is the finest quality grain reserved for the king.'
  • 天故专生此一流飧糠核而饱秭稗之人 - Literally 'Heaven deliberately creates this class of people who eat chaff and are content with秕,' meaning 'Heaven creates this class of people who are satisfied with low-quality food.'
  • 琐屑考据 - Literally 'trivial考证,' meaning 'meticulous考证.'
  • 迂阔讲学 - Literally 'remote and broad讲学,' meaning 'abstract and impractical讲学.'
  • 各就所长 - Literally 'each according to his strengths,' meaning 'each person excels in their own area.'
  • 自成一队 - Literally 'form their own队,' meaning 'form their own group.'
  • 孔圣、如来、老聃空中相遇 - Literally 'Confucius, Buddha, and Laozi meet in the air,' referring to the legendary meeting of three great sages.
  • 彼此微笑，一拱而过，绝不交言 - Literally 'they smile at each other, bow, and pass without speaking,' meaning 'they acknowledge each other but do not engage in conversation.'
  • 此天地之所以为大也 - Literally 'this is why the heavens and earth are great,' meaning 'this is the reason for the greatness of the universe.'

  • 半仙为禳解之 - '半仙' refers to a semi-spiritual or semi-divine figure, often a folk healer or shaman. '禳解' means to dispel or alleviate misfortune or evil influences.

  • 病者获安，李即辞去，从不受谢 - '病者获安' means the patient is cured or relieved. '李即辞去' means the practitioner (presumably a healer) leaves immediately. '从不受谢' means the healer does not accept any gratitude or reward.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 赴省過余 - Visited me on the way to the provincial administration; *赴省* refers to traveling to the provincial government office; *過余* means to visit the speaker.
  • 留飲 - Stayed for a drink or remained for a banquet; a common expression in classical Chinese to indicate a social visit with food and drink.

  • 两雄相偶 - Two males pairing together; a humorous or ironic way of describing the behavior.

  • 同服琉璜效验各别 - When taken together, the effects of cinnabar (shu huang) vary; *同服* means taking together; *效验* means effect or outcome; *各别* means different or distinct.
  • 琉璜有毒，从所知 - Cinnabar is poisonous, as is known; *从所知* is a classical way of saying 'as is known' or 'according to what is known.'
  • 疽以于背、于颈死者有之 - Some have died from abscesses on the back or neck; *疽* is an abscess or carbuncle; *于背、于颈* means 'on the back, on the neck.'
  • 祸福互异 - Fortunes and misfortunes differ; this implies that the outcomes are varied.

  • 托冢宰庸于冬至日嚼雪吞冰 - The person entrusted with the administration (the Grand Minister) named Yong chewed snow and ice on the winter solstice; *托冢宰* is a title or honorific for a high-ranking official.
  • 自称阳脏故然 - He claimed it was because of his yang constitution; *阳脏* refers to a yang constitution, a traditional Chinese medical term.
  • 尹文瑞公隆冬不戴貂帽 - Grand Secretary Yin Wenrui did not wear a sable hat in the depth of winter; *貂帽* is a sable hat, a symbol of status and warmth.
  • 戴则虽大雪中汗出如雨 - If he wore it, he would sweat like rain even in heavy snow; this is a humorous contrast.
  • 宋夏英公服钟乳、琉璜 - Song Dynasty's Xia Yinggong took cinnabar and stalactites (zhong ru); *钟乳* is stalactites or a medicinal substance; *琉璜* is cinnabar.
  • 偶离此二味，则手足如冰 - If he missed these two, his hands and feet would be as cold as ice; this is a hyperbolic way of describing the effects of the medicine.
  • 杭州王事师林常服琉璜 - Mr. Lin, the official in charge of Hangzhou, regularly took cinnabar.
  • 毛孔中常突起小泡，青烟一道，直射而出 - Small bubbles would rise from his pores, and a column of blue smoke would shoot out; this is a vivid and somewhat fantastical description of the effects of cinnabar.
  • 据云其毒从毛孔中出，便无他患 - It is said that the poison was expelled through the pores, and thus there were no other ailments; *据云* is 'it is said.'
  • 至今其人年高，卒无恙云 - To this day, the man is old, and has finally suffered no harm; *卒无恙* means 'finally suffered no harm.'

  • 明日报其方 - Means 'the next day, he reported the prescription.'
  • 果两剂而愈 - Means 'indeed, it cured him after two doses.'
  • 宰香河时 - Refers to the time when the official served as the magistrate of Xianghe.
  • 有老翁率其女来喊冤 - Means 'an old man brought his daughter to lodge a complaint.'
  • 女颇的姿 - Means 'the daughter was quite beautiful.'
  • 何冤？ - Means 'what is your complaint?'
  • 女为城隍神所据 - Means 'my daughter is possessed by the city god.'
  • 每夜神以车来迎，便痴迷不醒 - Means 'every night, the god comes in a carriage to take her, and she becomes dazed and unconscious.'
  • 必到次日辰刻，才放女归 - Means 'he only lets her return home at the third watch of the next day.'
  • 女已定婚某家，致某家不敢来娶 - Means 'my daughter is already betrothed to a certain family, but that family dares not come to marry her.'
  • 公肯一言，或神肯听亦未可知 - Means 'if you speak a word, perhaps the god will listen.'
  • 公窃喜自负 - Means 'the official secretly felt pleased and confident.'
  • 即作文书交翁焚而投之 - Means 'he immediately wrote a document and gave it to the old man to burn and throw it.'
  • 次日，翁果同女来谢 - Means 'the next day, the old man indeed came with his daughter to thank him.'
  • 昨晚神竟不来迎女矣 - Means 'the god did not come to take my daughter last night.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 城隍 - Refers to the local city god, a deity in traditional Chinese religion responsible for protecting the city and its inhabitants.
  • 上帝 - Refers to the Supreme Deity, often associated with the highest god in Chinese mythology.
  • 辰刻 - Refers to the third watch of the night, which is from 7 PM to 9 PM.
  • 文书 - Refers to a written document, often used in official or formal contexts.

Proper Nouns

  • 良乡 - The name of the district where the official first served.
  • 香河 - The name of the district where the official later served.
  • 城隍 - The name of the city god.
  • 上帝 - The name of the Supreme Deity.

[201]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 官运二则 - Title meaning 'Two Stories of Official Fortune,' indicating the text is a collection of anecdotes about officials' fortunes.
  • 料理齐全 - To manage or prepare everything thoroughly.
  • 挂彩张灯 - Decorated with colorful decorations and lanterns, common practice in traditional Chinese culture to welcome important guests.
  • 权将手本留下 - Temporarily keep the official document.
  • 为日后图报之地 - For future opportunities to repay.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 钦差 - A high-ranking official sent by the emperor to handle important matters, often with special authority.
  • 巡抚 - A provincial governor in the Qing dynasty, responsible for both civil and military affairs.
  • 手本 - An official document used for formal introductions or requests.
  • 牲牢夫役 - Livestock and servants, indicative of the elaborate preparations made for important visitors, reflecting the social hierarchy and etiquette of the time.
  • 被罪之人 - Refers to someone who has been punished or disgraced, often due to political or legal issues.

Proper Nouns

  • 华雍 - The name of the local official.
  • 淮宁 - The place where the official serves.
  • 张荣 - The capable servant.
  • 江西巡抚阿公思哈 - The governor of Jiangxi Province, named A Gong Sihua.
  • 华令 - The local official (华雍).

[202]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 未二年 - Refers to 'not two years later' or 'within two years,' indicating a short period of time.
  • 起用 - Means 'to appoint' or 'to reappoint,' often used in the context of officials being recalled to service.
  • 山西巡抚 - Refers to the governor of Shanxi province, a high-ranking official in the Qing dynasty.
  • 华四参限满 - Indicates that the official named Huá Sì had completed his term of service in the military or civil administration.
  • 送部引见 - Means 'to be sent to the Ministry for an audience with the emperor,' a formal procedure for officials to present themselves to the emperor.
  • 奉旨发往山西 - Means 'by imperial decree, to be sent to Shanxi,' indicating an official appointment.
  • 初次到辕禀谒 - Means 'on his first visit to the governor's office to pay respects,' describing the initial meeting.
  • 如得至宝 - Means 'as if he had found a treasure,' expressing great delight or surprise.
  • 遣家人致意司道 - Means 'to send a servant to convey a message to the provincial officials,' indicating a formal communication.
  • 请大老爷缓见 - Means 'to ask the governor to delay the audience,' requesting a postponement.
  • 我主恩人到矣 - Means 'my master's benefactor has arrived,' expressing gratitude and respect.
  • 开中门 - Means 'to open the central gate,' a gesture of welcome and importance.
  • 亲迎至堂下 - Means 'to personally welcome him to the hall,' showing great respect and hospitality.
  • 呼老贤弟 - Means 'to call him old brother,' a term of familiarity and respect.
  • 握手入内 - Means 'to take his hand and lead him inside,' a gesture of warmth and friendship.
  • 罗列酒肴 - Means 'to arrange wine and food,' preparing a feast.
  • 待如上客 - Means 'to treat him as a guest of honor,' showing high regard.
  • 长跪辞谢 - Means 'to kneel and decline,' expressing humility and gratitude.
  • 惧不敢当 - Means 'fearful and unable to accept,' showing modesty.
  • 有恩不报 - Means 'to not repay a favor,' expressing a sense of duty.
  • 我是何等人耶 - Means 'what kind of person am I?' expressing self-reflection.
  • 今日我尽我心 - Means 'today I will do my best,' expressing commitment.
  • 明日汝行汝礼 - Means 'tomorrow you will follow your own customs,' indicating a return to normalcy.
  • 尽欢痛饮 - Means 'to drink heartily,' describing a joyful and festive atmosphere.
  • 送上轿而别 - Means 'to see him off in a sedan chair,' indicating a formal departure.
  • 司道闻之，莫不刮目 - Means 'the provincial officials, upon hearing this, all looked at him with new respect,' expressing admiration.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 山西巡抚 - The governor of Shanxi province, a significant administrative position during the Qing dynasty.
  • 华四 - The name of the official, likely a real historical figure or a representative of a class of officials.
  • 阿公 - A respectful term for an elder or a senior official, possibly a high-ranking governor or a noble.
  • 司道 - Refers to provincial officials, including governors, military commanders, and other high-ranking officials.
  • 长跪 - A traditional form of kneeling, often used in formal settings to show respect and humility.
  • 尽欢痛饮 - A common practice in Chinese culture to celebrate and show hospitality.
  • 刮目 - An idiom meaning to look at someone with new respect or admiration.

Proper Nouns

  • 阿公 - A respectful term for an elder or a senior official.
  • 华四 - The name of the official.
  • 山西巡抚 - The position of governor of Shanxi province.
  • 司道 - Provincial officials, including governors, military commanders, and other high-ranking officials.
